skill.name: golang-code-style
skill.description: Guides Go code style decisions for readable functions, declarations, control flow, line breaks, and comments. Use it when writing or reviewing Go code.
skill.summary: Apply practical Go style rules for clearer code reviews and implementations.
skill.author: samber

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and cryptography alerts, but review found they are Markdown, JSON prompt text, and Go examples. The only retained risk factor is constrained Bash access for normal Go, linter, and git workflows. No prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code, Go fenced examples, and JSON prompt strings. They do not execute commands or instruct the agent to run shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation or test prompt text, not executable Ruby or shell code. No evidence of command construction or shell execution was found at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations do not define cryptographic algorithms or unsafe cipher choices. They are style guidance, references, or evaluation prompts that mention Go code structures.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar implementation. One prompt mentions tls.Config generically, without recommending weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Project Metadata
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a homepage value in metadata. The skill does not include code or instructions that fetch from this URL or send data to it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as homepage metadata. Search found no curl, wget, fetch, net/http request, or other network exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Constrained Bash Tool Access Requires Normal Review Caution
low_findings.description: The skill allows Bash commands for go, golangci-lint, and git, plus Agent use. This is appropriate for Go style review, but it is still an external command capability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration directly grants constrained command prefixes. The prefixes are relevant to Go development and do not show malicious command intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Capability Declared
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares constrained Bash access for Go, golangci-lint, and git commands. This is not malicious, but users should expect local tooling commands during reviews.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in allowed-tools. The risk is low because the command prefixes are narrow and match the skill purpose.

content.value_statement: Go style decisions can be inconsistent across reviews and generated code. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete rules for readable Go.
actual_capabilities: Guides line breaks for long calls, signatures, and argument lists.
Chooses var or short declarations based on zero-value intent.
Recommends early returns, shallow control flow, and switch statements.
Reviews slice, map, and struct literal initialization style.
Advises on function size, parameter order, and options structs.
Explains when comments improve clarity or hide unclear code.
limitations: It does not replace gofmt, goimports, or golangci-lint.
It does not cover identifier naming conventions in depth.
It does not provide full architecture or design pattern guidance.
Some rules require project context and human judgment.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Find style issues that automated formatters do not catch, such as nesting, parameter lists, and unclear conditions.

use_cases.title: Generate Cleaner Go Code
use_cases.description: Ask an AI coding assistant to write Go that follows practical readability conventions from the first draft.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Guidance
use_cases.description: Create consistent review comments for line breaks, declarations, collection initialization, and function shape.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Style Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one Go file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Go file for code style. Focus on readability, line breaks, declarations, collections, and control flow.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor For Clarity
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve an existing implementation without changing behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Go code for clearer style. Preserve behavior and explain each readability change in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Team Standards
prompt_templates.scenario: Align new code with shared conventions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write this Go feature using the project style rules. Use early returns, named struct fields, initialized collections, and short focused functions.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Repository Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Review a larger Go codebase for style consistency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Go repository for style consistency. Group findings by control flow, function design, declarations, collections, and file organization.

output_examples.input: A Go handler contains deeply nested validation and a long helper call.
output_examples.output: The review recommends early returns, breaking the helper call by argument, and keeping the success path at shallow indentation.

output_examples.input: A constructor explicitly assigns zero values and returns nil collections.
output_examples.output: The response explains which zero values can be omitted and which slices or maps should be initialized as empty collections.

output_examples.input: A service function accepts many independent parameters.
output_examples.output: The review suggests placing context first and grouping related configuration into an options struct.

best_practices: Run formatters and linters before asking for judgment-based style review.
Share nearby project conventions when reviewing code from an existing repository.
Ask for behavior-preserving edits when style changes should not alter logic.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the skill to override gofmt or project lint rules.
Do not use it as a substitute for security, performance, or architecture review.
Do not request broad rewrites when a targeted style review is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill replace gofmt?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on judgment-based style decisions that formatters and basic linters do not cover.

faq.question: Can it review existing Go code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify readability issues and suggest behavior-preserving style improvements.

faq.question: Does it cover naming conventions?
faq.answer: Only lightly. The source skill points to a separate Go naming skill for detailed naming guidance.

faq.question: Will it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It may use constrained Go, golangci-lint, or git commands when the host tool allows them.

faq.question: Is it useful for generated code?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce clearer Go code in the first draft.

faq.question: What should I provide for best results?
faq.answer: Provide the Go file, related project conventions, and whether you want review comments or direct edits.
